evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go mixes cross-cutting protocol action declarations (`ProtocolActionSpecs`) with upload-domain-specific action policy (`UploadActionSpec`, payload rules, and `UploadActionSpecs`) in one catalog file.

The same file adds adapter glue (`UploadActionFamilySpecs`) to down-convert richer upload specs back to generic `familyspec.ActionSpec`, indicating competing abstraction levels in a single ownership unit.

goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go and goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go both validate the same upload/C2C contract relationships from different package perspectives, reflecting boundary coupling that is verified through cross-package matrix tests instead of a single clear ownership seam.

Global default runtimes/controllers are duplicated across modules (broadcast.DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime in goseg/broadcast/state.go, defaultEventRuntime in goseg/docker/events/events.go, defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime in goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go), creating hidden cross-module state dependencies.

Runtime assembly depends on very wide callback bags instead of narrower package contracts (goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go Runtime*Ops groups; goseg/importer/importer.go importerRuntime; goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go workflowRuntime), so integration correctness is deferred to runtime validation.

Runtime context defaults couple to docker path probing in defaults.DockerData (goseg/defaults/defaults.go) and are consumed by artifact-writing paths (goseg/config/runtimecontext/context.go + goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go), increasing environment-sensitive dependency behavior.

Event publication seams can bind to stale brokers because DefaultEventRuntime returns a value snapshot (goseg/docker/events/events.go) and callers cache it in runtime constructors (goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go newWorkflowRuntime, goseg/importer/importer.go defaultImporterRuntime).

Startup orchestration composes services through large callback maps (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go serviceCallbacks/startupServiceSpecs), making boundary behavior dependent on name-keyed wiring rather than typed seams.

Transition and broadcast pipelines use separate ad-hoc buses and backpressure policies across modules (goseg/broadcast/state.go schedulePackBus, goseg/docker/events/events.go publishDropOnFull, goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go errEventBusFull), increasing cross-boundary inconsistency.

Fail-fast service health monitoring spawns goroutines that block on service error channels without context cancellation in waitForFailFastErrors (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go), creating long-lived leaked goroutines for healthy long-running services.

Control flow around runtime preparation is repeated with similar nil/validation boilerplate across modules (goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go prepareForUse, goseg/importer/importer.go validateImporter*Runtime, goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go resolveWorkflowRuntime), increasing local indirection cost.

Write-path fallback logic depends on inferred defaults and multi-stage retries (goseg/docker/orchestration/internal/artifactwriter/artifact_writer.go Write and goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go WriteWgConf), which is robust but internally complex for a single artifact write path.
All five reviewed files are package-level `doc.go` comments with concise domain context and no verbose entry/exit logging, no defensive boilerplate branches, and no pass-through wrapper code.
`goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go` includes a policy pointer (`docs/architecture/contracts-governance.md`) plus contract-specific constraints, which is useful but still partly formulaic across package docs.
The package docs in `goseg/shipworkflow/doc.go`, `goseg/startram/doc.go`, and `goseg/system/doc.go` follow a similar two-paragraph template pattern, creating mild uniform prose ceremony.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: Several package docs read as templated statements with repeated structure; tighten wording to keep only non-obvious constraints and remove formulaic phrasing.

evidence: No `TODO/FIXME` migration markers, `deprecated` markers, or explicit legacy/new dual-path notes are present in any of the five files.
`goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go` points to a canonical governance policy, indicating consolidation rather than transitional shim language.

No compatibility-shim language or temporary removal markers are present in `goseg/shipworkflow/doc.go`, `goseg/startram/doc.go`, `goseg/system/doc.go`, or `goseg/protocol/contracts/familycatalog/catalog.go`.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: These files are documentation-only surfaces; migration completeness outside this slice cannot be validated from this batch alone.

evidence: holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows very large flat directories with mixed concerns: goseg/config/ has 41 files, goseg/docker/orchestration/ has 34 files, and goseg/shipworkflow/ has 55 files.

In reviewed files, unrelated responsibilities co-exist at the same directory level: bootstrap lifecycle (config/bootstrap.go), runtime context accessors (config/config.go), transport handling (shipworkflow/newship.go), and infrastructure runtime wiring (shipworkflow/newship_service.go).

docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go and docker/orchestration/container_config.go are placement outliers in orchestration root while primarily serving container-runtime adaptation and container-type config mapping.
`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` uses `WiFi` acronym casing in exported API (`WiFiRuntimeService`, `IsWiFiEnabled`, `StartWiFiInfoLoop`).
`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go` mixes `WiFi` and `Wifi` in the same runtime surface (`NewWifiClient`, `WifiInfoTicker`, `wifiNmcli*`), creating naming drift inside one subsystem.
`goseg/routines/system/system.go` has clear cancellation-aware loops (`runRoutineWithContext`, `runChopWithContext`) with explicit `ctx.Done()` exits.

`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` short-circuits `connectInfoLoop` when a loop is already running (`wifiInfoLoopStop != nil`) without exposing whether refresh ownership changed, which can hide caller intent.
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: Some control-flow branches intentionally no-op (already-running loop path) but provide no explicit status to callers, reducing operational clarity.

evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` exposes `UpdateUrbitSectionFn` as `func(any) error`, erasing section type information at the runtime boundary.
`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` persists section updates through `persistShipUrbitSection(... mutateFn func(any) error ...)`, so wrong section/mutator pairing is only caught at runtime.
`README.md` states websocket action canonical constants live in `groundseg/protocol/actions` and references `groundseg/protocol/actions.ActionC2CConnect`.

Current contract code/tests use `groundseg/protocol/contracts/...` (`goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go`, `goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go`), indicating documentation-to-runtime contract drift.
`goseg/config/bootstrap.go` relies on package-global mutable state (`globalConfig`, `Ready`, `GSContainers`, `initOnce`), making behavior dependent on prior process init sequence.
`goseg/importer/importer.go` stores request-critical runtime state in globals (`uploadSessions`, `uploadDir`, `tempDir`) initialized out-of-band by `Initialize`.
`goseg/broadcast/state.go` and `goseg/docker/events/events.go` both maintain mutable process-wide default runtimes via `SetDefault...`/`ResetDefault...`, coupling test/runtime order across packages.

Most reviewed packages use lowercase-wrapped error text, but `goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go` emits capitalized messages (`"Couldn't ..."`, `"Unknown NewShip action"`) that diverge from prevailing error style.
The same subsystem uses both acronym styles (`WiFi` vs `Wifi`) across adjacent files (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`).
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: Style and naming conventions are mostly consistent globally, but there are localized style islands in wifi/newship flows.

evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/container_config.go` exposes both typed (`ContainerConfigForType`) and string-cast (`ContainerConfigForTypeString`) entrypoints for the same API, creating dual call patterns.

`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` keeps typed generic helpers (`persistShipUrbitSectionConfig[T]`) but still routes through untyped `func(any) error` seams, indicating partially coherent API boundaries.
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: API direction is generally improving toward typed seams, but legacy untyped/string adapters remain on public paths.

evidence: `goseg/importer/importer.go` enforces upload authorization with token headers plus `ValidateUploadSessionTokenFn` checks in `validateUploadRequest` and `setUploadSessionWithRuntime`.

`goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go` exported action handlers (`HandleNewShip`, `HandleNewShipBoot`, `CancelNewShip`) execute provisioning/deletion paths without an explicit auth context or token parameter at this boundary.

findings.summary: Action catalog combines protocol registry and upload domain policy, blurring ownership boundaries.
evidence: `ProtocolActionSpecs()` and `UploadActionSpecs()` coexist in the same catalog module even though one is generic protocol registry and the other encodes upload-specific payload policy.
`UploadActionFamilySpecs()` exists only to project upload-specific structs back into generic action specs, showing a leaky boundary between domain policy and protocol catalog.

Conformance and uploadsvc tests both need to assert canonical upload/C2C contract alignment, which suggests contract ownership is spread across package seams rather than centered behind one authoritative interface.
findings.suggestion: Split `catalog/action` into explicit ownership layers: keep a pure protocol action registry in one package/file, move upload payload-policy specs to an upload-domain contract package, and expose one canonical read API (used by both conformance and uploadsvc tests) so cross-package matrix validation can be reduced to validating that single seam.

findings.summary: Runtime constructors capture EventRuntime snapshots by value, so broker swaps do not propagate across seams.
evidence: DefaultEventRuntime() returns EventRuntime by value in goseg/docker/events/events.go.
newWorkflowRuntime() stores defaultEventRuntime := events.DefaultEventRuntime() and emits via that captured value.
defaultImporterRuntime() wires PublishImportTransitionFn from events.DefaultEventRuntime().PublishImportShipTransition, also snapshot-based.
SetDefaultEventRuntime replaces the global runtime later, but already-constructed runtimes continue publishing on previously captured channels.
findings.suggestion: Return and pass an EventBroker reference (pointer/interface) from a single composition root, and inject it into workflow/importer constructors so runtime swaps are explicit and consistent.

findings.summary: Multiple package-level default runtimes/controllers create hidden mutable dependencies and split-brain behavior risks.
evidence: broadcast has mutable global defaults (DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime/SetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime).
broadcast loop uses a separate mutable default controller (defaultBroadcastLoopController).
events package has an independent mutable default runtime (defaultEventRuntime).
docker subsystem has its own default runtime instance (defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime).
These defaults are independently replaceable and can diverge across modules, increasing hidden coupling and test/runtime order sensitivity.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single top-level runtime container built at startup and pass explicit dependencies into subsystem constructors; keep package-level defaults only as thin legacy adapters.

findings.summary: Very wide callback bags degrade seam clarity and shift integration failures from compile time to runtime.
evidence: RuntimeContainerOps/RuntimeSnapshotOps/RuntimeUrbitOps aggregate many unrelated callbacks in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go.
importerRuntime embeds multiple large runtime groups and relies on runtime string-tag validation plus many missing-dependency error sentinels.
workflowRuntime embeds several orchestration runtime bags directly, mixing lifecycle, eventing, config snapshots, and container control at one seam.
startup orchestrator uses name-keyed service callback maps that further widen and loosen integration boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Refactor runtimes into smaller capability interfaces per use-case (e.g., transition publisher, container lifecycle, urbit config mutation), and inject only required capabilities per flow to reduce nil-runtime and wiring drift.

findings.summary: Fail-fast health checking spawns goroutines that can block indefinitely on service error channels.
evidence: waitForFailFastErrors creates one goroutine per fail-fast service that does a blocking receive from monitored.err.
For healthy long-running services, monitored.err does not close until shutdown, so those goroutines remain blocked.
The receive goroutines are not canceled via timeout context, only the caller select returns on timeout.
findings.suggestion: Remove per-call goroutines and use non-blocking/timeout-aware selects directly on service channels, or track a one-time watcher per service with lifecycle tied to startup context cancellation.

findings.summary: Large flat directories obscure ownership and make change paths unpredictable.
evidence: Directory profiles show config (41 files), docker/orchestration (34), and shipworkflow (55), each above the >10 mixed-concern split threshold in this review rubric.
Reviewed files from these directories represent different layers (bootstrap/runtime context/domain workflow/container orchestration) co-located without stronger folder boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create focused subfolders `goseg/config/bootstrap/`, `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/containerbridge/`; (2) move files in order with `git mv goseg/config/bootstrap.go goseg/config/bootstrap/bootstrap.go`, `git mv goseg/shipworkflow/newship.go goseg/shipworkflow/newship/handler.go`, `git mv goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go goseg/shipworkflow/newship/runtime.go`, `git mv goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go goseg/docker/orchestration/containerbridge/runtime_bridge.go`, `git mv goseg/docker/orchestration/container_config.go goseg/docker/orchestration/containerbridge/config_builder.go`; (3) update package names/imports with `goimports -w` (or `gofmt` plus manual import edits); (4) validate with `go test ./goseg/config/... ./goseg/shipworkflow/... ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...` and `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Container adapter/build logic is misplaced in orchestration root instead of container-focused boundary package.
evidence: container_bridge.go is a runtime-input projection and adapter layer for Llama/Netdata/Minio container runtimes rather than general orchestration entry behavior.
container_config.go is type-to-builder dispatch for container-specific config constructors, but it lives alongside broad orchestration runtime files in the same root directory.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/docker/orchestration/containerbridge/`; (2) move bridge and config dispatcher first, then tests (`git mv .../container_bridge.go .../containerbridge/runtime_bridge.go`, `git mv .../container_config.go .../containerbridge/config_builder.go`, `git mv .../container_bridge_test.go .../containerbridge/runtime_bridge_test.go`); (3) keep a thin compatibility shim in old package temporarily that delegates to new package; (4) update call sites via `rg -n "ContainerConfigForType|llamaRuntimeFromDocker|netdataRuntimeFromDocker|minioRuntimeFromDocker" goseg`; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...`.

findings.summary: New-ship flow blends transport parsing, domain policy, and infra wiring in top-level workflow files.
evidence: HandleNewShip in newship.go parses websocket payload actions and also coordinates cancel/reset behavior directly, while HandleNewShipBoot performs validation, drive prep, event publication, and async provisioning launch.

newship_service.go constructs provisioning runtime by directly binding config, docker/network orchestration, and startram operations, so boundary ownership between workflow orchestration and infrastructure composition is unclear.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) split `newship.go` into `newship/transport_handler.go` (action decode/dispatch), `newship/validator.go` (patp/key/drive validation), and `newship/usecase.go` (orchestration flow); (2) move infrastructure binding from `newship_service.go` into `newship/runtimebinding/` with explicit interfaces consumed by usecase layer; (3) update imports and exported entrypoints (`HandleNewShip`, `HandleNewShipBoot`) to thin façade functions; (4) run `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` and then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Contract governance is fragmented across docs, catalog specs, and tests without one authoritative code surface.
evidence: README.md declares governance and quality-gate contract expectations, but runnable enforcement shown here is spread across two separate test packages.
Action and StarTram contract families use different local composition patterns and do not expose a unified governance registry from the reviewed files.
Package documentation points to policy docs, but change safety still depends on engineers discovering multiple files across protocol/contracts and uploadsvc.
findings.suggestion: Create a single governance entrypoint in protocol/contracts that exports all family specs plus required conformance checks, then have README.md and uploadsvc/doc.go reference that entrypoint directly (not only prose docs). Keep tests focused on verifying the entrypoint output rather than reconstructing policy in multiple places.

findings.summary: Contract policy placement is scattered across catalog, conformance, uploadsvc tests, and root docs.
evidence: Governance-relevant files for one concern are distributed across four directory areas: root docs, protocol/contracts/catalog, protocol/contracts/conformance, and uploadsvc.
directory_profiles show separate small folders for action, startram, and conformance, but no reviewed in-tree index file ties them into a navigable contract-policy package boundary.
uploadsvc test coverage doubles as cross-package governance validation, which blurs folder ownership between runtime behavior tests and contract-governance conformance.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) add `goseg/protocol/contracts/governance/` with `registry.go` and `conformance_policy.go` that aggregate family specs and required payload-rule assertions; (2) move `catalog/action/specs.go` and `catalog/startram/specs.go` behind the governance registry API while keeping family files as leaf declarations; (3) relocate cross-package governance assertions from `goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go` into `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/` and keep uploadsvc tests runtime-local. After each stage run `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts/...`, then `cd goseg && go test ./uploadsvc/...`, then `cd goseg && go test ./...` to validate imports and behavior.

findings.summary: Core services rely on mutable package-level singletons and init order, creating hidden boot/test coupling.
evidence: Config/bootstrap gates state through `initOnce` plus globals (`Ready`, `globalConfig`, `GSContainers`).
Importer request path depends on global `uploadDir`/`tempDir`/`uploadSessions` populated by prior `Initialize` call.
Broadcast/events each expose mutable default runtimes via `SetDefault...`, so behavior can vary by prior caller mutation.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit application-scoped runtime structs (config state, importer session store, event broker, broadcast state) and pass them through startup wiring; keep package defaults only as thin compatibility adapters.

findings.summary: Urbit section update seams erase compile-time types with `any`, allowing invalid mutator/section pairings until runtime.
evidence: Runtime contract declares `UpdateUrbitSectionFn func(patp string, section UrbitConfigSection, mutateFn func(any) error) error`.
`persistShipUrbitSection` forwards a `func(any) error`, so section-target compatibility is not enforced by the type system.
findings.suggestion: Replace the untyped section mutator seam with typed per-section callbacks (or a generic typed registry keyed by section enum) so invalid section/mutator combinations fail at compile time.

findings.summary: README contract guidance points to deprecated/incorrect action package paths, diverging from actual runtime contract modules.
evidence: README says canonical websocket action registry is `protocol/actions` and references `groundseg/protocol/actions.ActionC2CConnect`.
Current implementation/tests consume `groundseg/protocol/contracts` action bindings and catalog specs.
findings.suggestion: Update README contract section to the current `protocol/contracts` entrypoints and keep referenced constant paths synchronized with conformance tests.

findings.summary: Wi-Fi runtime naming alternates between `WiFi` and `Wifi`, reducing discoverability and grepability within one API family.
evidence: API file exports `WiFiRuntimeService`, `IsWiFiEnabled`, `StartWiFiInfoLoop`.
Runtime methods file defines fields like `NewWifiClient`/`WifiInfoTicker` and `wifiNmcli*` types, mixing acronym style in the same subsystem.
findings.suggestion: Normalize acronym casing to one convention (`WiFi` or `Wifi`) across the wifi runtime/service surface and update dependent call sites/tests together.

findings.summary: High-impact new-ship operations expose handler APIs without explicit authorization context while upload flows enforce token checks at boundary.
evidence: `validateUploadRequest` and session setup in importer require upload token IDs/hashes and authorization results.
`HandleNewShip` / `HandleNewShipBoot` / `CancelNewShip` accept payload/action input and trigger provisioning/deletion paths without auth token/session parameters at this API boundary.
findings.suggestion: Align boundary contracts by requiring an authenticated actor/session context for new-ship entrypoints (or enforce an explicit pre-authorized command type) and document that contract next to handler APIs.
